content:
Hello! I wanted to start work on zero-cost CStrings, however I wanted to use a ThinBox to hold the pointer. However since you guys have a smaller alloc crate it's missing. Currently it's a sad blank module, before i just copy and paste a giant blob for thinbox I wanted to ask why.

#[cfg(not(no_thin))]
#[unstable(feature = "thin_box", issue = "92791")]
pub use thin::ThinBox;

#[cfg(not(no_thin))]
mod thin;
It's empty because thin boxes were not required at the time when alloc was submitted to the kernel source.

Now then, if you want them to be added then you have to make sure that you have a consumer for it (in this case those zero-cost CStrings) and that if it does allocations those have to be fallible, i.e. they must be try_* methods.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Now then, if you want them to be added then you have to make sure that you have a consumer for it (in this case those zero-cost CStrings) and that if it does allocations those have to be fallible, i.e. they must be try_* methods.

what do you mean try methods?
Things like Box::try_new, Vec::try_resize, etc. All of those methods return a Result that has Error set to an allocation error.
This is essential because all of the allocations done by the kernel must be fallible.

That is to cover cases where allocs can fail, most commonly by running out of memory. It's expected for the kernel to handle those situations as gracefully as possible, so if it could propagate the alloc error it's the best option out there.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

That is to cover cases where allocs can fail, most commonly by running out of memory. It's expected for the kernel to handle those situations as gracefully as possible, so if it could propagate the alloc error it's the best option out there.

wow this is more complex than I thought, how would you suggest adding them?
FYI, ThinBox in std is almost definitely going to see some significant API rework. Its current design is still very useful for us as-is, but just be aware that the implementation may change

Carson Range said:

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

That is to cover cases where allocs can fail, most commonly by running out of memory. It's expected for the kernel to handle those situations as gracefully as possible, so if it could propagate the alloc error it's the best option out there.

wow this is more complex than I thought, how would you suggest adding them?

ThinBox is currently a wrapper around the internal WithHeader, with a few extra layers of indirection. WithHeader::new has a line alloc::handle_alloc_error that turns an allocation failure into a panic. You just need to add a WithHeader::try_new that propagates this error instead, and expose it as ThinBox::try_new
Okay do you have doc rs links to explain what that does? I'm getting lost in the weeds of why we are doing custom library standard datatypes again.

Carson Range said:

Okay do you have doc rs links to explain what that does? I'm getting lost in the weeds of why we are doing custom library standard datatypes again.

Just to reiterate: One reason is that we do not want allocation to panic. We want failed allocations to return an error instead. Upstream alloc does not provide this for all types.
Okay that makes sense, how do I create a WithHeader?

Carson Range said:

Okay that makes sense, how do I create a WithHeader?

Is this something from the rust std library because I couldn't find anything when I googled it. But I've been struggling with what is from std and what has the exact same names and is completely different within the kernel.
alloc in the kernel is basically std's alloc, with some more try_x methods to handle failed allocation and without some types that aren't needed

Carson Range said:

Okay that makes sense, how do I create a WithHeader?

This is in Rust's alloc source. You won't have results googling a lot of these things, they're implementation details

Google indexes the source code of the standard library, in fact! (Or at least a version of it) For me, googling does not give great results, but it does give me enough, including the official source view that defines WithHeader, plus the tracking issue. Even the RFL v8 is there :)

Miguel Ojeda said:

Google indexes the source code of the standard library, in fact! (Or at least a version of it) For me, googling does not give great results, but it does give me enough, including the official source view that defines WithHeader, plus the tracking issue. Even the RFL v8 is there :)

Do you mind sending the source then? I didn't see reprogramming the rust std when i join this group lol.
I zero results searching for WithHeader online or in the repo, google just gives a butch of http or packet libraries.
Have you looked at the implementation of ThinBox in std at all? The standard library docs (and all rustdoc docs) have a button that shows you the source file

Do you mind sending the source then? I didn't see reprogramming the rust std when i join this group lol.

Which source? If you mean alloc, it is in the Rust repository, under library. But please note that the changes required need to follow a particular convention -- please see how we have done the others in our fork.

To be clear, we are not "reprogramming the rust std" -- so far we are trying to keep it as close as possible to alloc, as the rationale in rust/alloc/README.md explains (please give it a look). Though it is true that we may need to fork it further in the future.

I zero results searching for WithHeader online or in the repo, google just gives a butch of http or packet libraries.

With a query in Google like:

rust "withheader"

you should see relevant results (for me, it does so in the first few results, even the RFL patch is in the first page).

And if you grep for WithHeader in the repository (the Rust one, not the kernel's), you will see it too.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I zero results searching for WithHeader online or in the repo, google just gives a butch of http or packet libraries.

With a query in Google like:

rust "withheader"

you should see relevant results (for me, it does so in the first few results, even the RFL patch is in the first page).

And if you grep for WithHeader in the repository (the Rust one, not the kernel's), you will see it too.

I was missing the quotes on my search, but i still dont see it in the repo. Maybe my fork is outdated, but since I changed orgin to wedson's I don't think I can actually pull the changes currently.

Trevor Gross said:

Have you looked at the implementation of ThinBox in std at all? The standard library docs (and all rustdoc docs) have a button that shows you the source file

not the source yet, just the normal doc page, looking at it now though.

What is the first step here, I dont have much experience with using Guards, or programming useful datatypes like this in general. But anything I can do to help, but I also don't want to make a mistake that affects everyone that uses the kernel's tinybox.

Carson Range said:

I also don't want to make a mistake that affects everyone that uses the kernel's tinybox.

Never worry about this sort of thing, this is why we have reviews :)

What exactly is your background? It seems like you might be a bit newer to the Rust ecosystem

Trevor Gross said:

Carson Range said:

I also don't want to make a mistake that affects everyone that uses the kernel's tinybox.

Never worry about this sort of thing, this is why we have reviews :)

What exactly is your background? It seems like you might be a bit newer to the Rust ecosystem

With Rust I've been working on a gui app with iced and doing a fullstack website with leptos and axum. But I took the os fundamentals and internals class so I do understand inodes and os concepts. It might not seem like it but this is not a normal rust project, even something like importing a datatype becomes hard because of the split from std's alloc.
It's true I only have like a year of rust experience, but Rust has only been around since 2011.

Yeah, nobody has really been working with Rust all that long. Anyway for this specific project it would definitely help to poke around the Rust library on github and just get a better feel for how things are laid out there. The source for rustc's alloc is at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/tree/master/library/alloc (the other big names, core, std, proc_macro, and test, live in the same parent directory). RFL has more or less forked the alloc/src directory to https://github.com/torvalds/linux/tree/master/rust/alloc. Miguel keeps it in sync with the upstream alloc library when we bump rust versions

But data types that haven't yet been needed aren't in RFL's alloc crate, e.g. ThinBox. So the first thing for your task is basically to sync ThinBox implementation from rust's alloc to RFL's alloc. Literally just copying the type over and making sure the kernel compiles still
Then you can build off of it

alloc and std seem pretty magical if you're not familiar with looking at the code on rust-lang/rust. But there's nothing special about them, they're just regular Cargo crates that happen to use a lot of unstable features, and extra attributes to make the compiler aware of the types.

Trevor Gross said:

Then you can build off of it

I thought I had to redo the sections that do allocation?
That's what I meant by building off of it. For a sanity check, just get the kernel with rust's ThinBox as-is first before you start changing the implementation.

Trevor Gross said:

That's what I meant by building off of it. For a sanity check, just get the kernel with rust's ThinBox as-is first before you start changing the implementation.

okay sounds good, what should I do next? focus on completing the task I want to work on or try to focus on changing it.

So I've progressed with my code, I think im making some good changes to CStr and CString. Now I need to use ThinBox and I looked into the code and learned about no_global_oom_handling, so I was wondering how do I create a ThinBox? Since I can't use either new method.

I also had questions about the disabling the ThinBox feature in the makefile here
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/f090f0d0eea9666a96702b29bc9a64cbabee85c5/rust/Makefile#L72

Since I'm using it now within kernel bindings does that mean that it's allowed? There are so many things I haven't had to deal with in rust I've learned here.
That attribute is actually added specific for the kernel’s fork of alloc, you’ll probably wind up being able to remove it

Trevor Gross said:

That attribute is actually added specific for the kernel’s fork of alloc, you’ll probably wind up being able to remove it

i assume you mean no_thin and not no_global_oom_handling
i rather repeat myself now than redraft a pr lol
no_thin, you probably just have to get remove that before you get ThinBox to show up at all

After that, just copy new to a new function called try_new that returns a Result. Then figure out where in the chain the allocation happens, and just make changes needed to propagate the error up instead of panicking

Trevor Gross said:

After that, just copy new to a new function called try_new that returns a Result. Then figure out where in the chain the allocation happens, and just make changes needed to propagate the error up instead of panicking

wow that's a lot easier than I thought.

Trevor Gross said:

ThinBox is currently a wrapper around the internal WithHeader, with a few extra layers of indirection. WithHeader::new has a line alloc::handle_alloc_error that turns an allocation failure into a panic. You just need to add a WithHeader::try_new that propagates this error instead, and expose it as ThinBox::try_new

yeah this has thrown me for a loop, I've looking through the docs and code for WithHeader. Do we have a common way to handle allocation? It seems natural to me that we would have our own layout and alloc_layout but I don't see anything like that.
Futher researching it seems WithHeader is the only type that has an alloc panic but I rather be safe than sorry.
There may be other functions that allocate, but just focus on new for now

Trevor Gross said:

There may be other functions that allocate, but just focus on new for now

Okay, for now I haven't included the errors for allocation, is there a doc or guidelines on how allocation is dealt with?

handle_alloc_error is what turns an alloc error into a panic in Rust's alloc. Kernel can never have a call to handle_alloc_error, so figure out where ThinBox has a call to handle_alloc_error and propagate that error instead (so whoever calls ThinBox::try_new can handle the error somehow). The signature should be the same as Box::try_new

So I've hit a road block, I've made changes to CString 

pub struct CString {
    buf: ThinBox<[u8]>,
    /// `NUL` terminator is counted in length.
    len: usize,
}

so I can translate between that and a CStr at o(1) however I forgot rust does not like slices that are of unknown lengths 

error[E0277]: the size for values of type `[u8]` cannot be known at compilation time
   --> rust/kernel/str.rs:617:42
    |
617 |             buf: ThinBox::try_new_unsize(cstr.0.buf),
    |                  ----------------------- ^^^^^^^^^^ doesn't have a size known at compile-time
    |                  |
    |                  required by a bound introduced by this call
    |
    = help: the trait `Sized` is not implemented for `[u8]`
note: required by a bound in `ThinBox::<Dyn>::try_new_unsize`
   --> /home/redhawk/code/linux/rust-for-linux/rust/alloc/boxed/thin.rs:114:27
    |
114 |     pub fn try_new_unsize<T>(value: T) -> Self
    |                           ^ required by this bound in `ThinBox::<Dyn>::try_new_unsize`

error[E0277]: the trait bound `[u8]: Unsize<[u8]>` is not satisfied
   --> rust/kernel/str.rs:617:42
    |
617 |             buf: ThinBox::try_new_unsize(cstr.0.buf),
    |                  ----------------------- ^^^^^^^^^^ the trait `Unsize<[u8]>` is not implemented for `[u8]`
    |                  |
    |                  required by a bound introduced by this call
    |
    = note: all implementations of `Unsize` are provided automatically by the compiler, see <https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/marker/trait.Unsize.html> for more information
note: required by a bound in `ThinBox::<Dyn>::try_new_unsize`
   --> /home/redhawk/code/linux/rust-for-linux/rust/alloc/boxed/thin.rs:116:12
    |
114 |     pub fn try_new_unsize<T>(value: T) -> Self
    |            -------------- required by a bound in this associated function
115 |     where
116 |         T: Unsize<Dyn>,
    |            ^^^^^^^^^^^ required by this bound in `ThinBox::<Dyn>::try_new_unsize`

It doesn't seem possible for me to use a thinbox for this. I was thinking something like Vec::from_raw_parts but that's extremely unsafe and I thought we were trying to avoid that.

let me know what you guys think of this since this idea hasn't really worked out

https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.from_raw_parts

The idea is to keep the size small. ThinBox<[u8]> is one pointer, Box<[u8]> is two pointers, and Vec<[u8]> is three.

I don't know what exactly you are doing but ThinBox<[u8]> keeps the length already, in the allocation https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=4bef56cb1cf4d470711e3debf0e4a57e

Trevor Gross said:

The idea is to keep the size small. ThinBox<[u8]> is one pointer, Box<[u8]> is two pointers, and Vec<[u8]> is three.

I don't know what exactly you are doing but ThinBox<[u8]> keeps the length already, in the allocation https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=4bef56cb1cf4d470711e3debf0e4a57e

I had no idea it kept track of length, I thought it was a unsized collection. The doc's didn't even have len(), im surprised. Also what type of list can I use here to transfer that into the thinbox buffer?

https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/src/kernel/str.rs.html#550-580
It's the same as Box<[u8]> - len doesn't come from the Box itself. Box<T> (and ThinBox<T>, Arc, others) have a Deref impl so you can use methods as if it was a &T -> &[u8] in this case

Trevor Gross said:

It's the same as Box<[u8]> - len doesn't come from the Box itself. Box<T> (and ThinBox<T>, Arc, others) have a Deref impl so you can use methods as if it was a &T -> &[u8] in this case

I didn't think Deref was where len came from, interesting
Deref is also how you can pretend a &String works as a &str, &Vec<T> works a &[T}, CString/CStr, PathBuf/Path etc

[u8] is unsized, Box<[u8]> is sized as two pointers (start + len), aka "fat pointer" - so pointer plus metadata. A Box<dyn SomeTrait> is also two pointers, as is &[u8] and &dyn SomeTrait.

ThinBox has the same information as Box except that metadata is stored at the beginning of the allocation instead of wherever the Box is. Benefits: now you only have one pointer to pass around, which makes it easier to work with some APIs that give you a single pointer for object storage (Wedson's use case). Downsides: knowing the length now means you need to spend time dereferencing another value
